metadata.assessed_by: AWS Transform Custom — Agentic Readiness Assessment
metadata.archetype_justification: Code-generation toolkit distributed as CLI JAR, Docker image, and build plugins. Optional online Spring Boot service is a stateless wrapper with no persistent data store, no authentication, and no user-specific state.
metadata.context: Code-generation toolkit that produces clients/servers from OpenAPI specs.

classification.rule_matched: 0 High, ≥2 Medium, ≥3 safety-impact Medium → Pilot-Ready (Safety Concerns)

findings.title: No scoped permissions — all endpoints publicly accessible
findings.description: The online service has no authentication or authorization mechanism. All endpoints are publicly accessible with no identity differentiation. CORS is configured as wide-open (allowedOrigins '*').
findings.gap: No scoped permission model exists. Any caller (including a potential agent) would have identical unrestricted access to all endpoints.
findings.recommendation: Deploy behind AWS API Gateway with resource-level IAM policies that restrict agent identities to specific endpoints (e.g., GET-only for read-only agents).

compensating_controls: Deploy the online service behind an API Gateway with IAM-based authorization and usage plans that scope agent access to read-only GET endpoints.
Use network-level controls (security groups, VPC) to restrict access to the online service during pilot.

findings.title: No action-level authorization — GET and POST equally accessible
findings.description: The online service has no authorization controls of any kind. There is no distinction between read operations (GET) and write operations (POST). Any caller can invoke any endpoint.
findings.gap: No action-level authorization exists. An agent intended for read-only access could invoke code-generation (POST) endpoints without restriction.
findings.recommendation: Add API Gateway method-level authorization or Spring Security configuration to differentiate read (GET) from write (POST) operations at the API layer.

compensating_controls: Deploy behind API Gateway with method-level authorization (allow GET, deny POST for read-only agent identities).
Implement a lightweight Spring Security filter that checks a request header/token for action-level permissions.

findings.title: No rate limiting — CPU-intensive endpoints unprotected
findings.description: The online service has no rate limiting at any layer. POST endpoints trigger CPU-intensive code generation which could be abused by a runaway agent loop.
findings.gap: No rate limiting exists. A runaway agent could overwhelm the service by calling POST endpoints repeatedly, causing CPU exhaustion and denial of service.
findings.recommendation: Deploy behind AWS API Gateway with usage plans that enforce rate limits (low burst for POST code-generation endpoints, higher for GET metadata endpoints).

compensating_controls: Deploy behind API Gateway with usage plans and throttle settings (e.g., 10 requests/second burst, 5 requests/second steady-state for POST endpoints).
Add a reverse proxy (nginx, HAProxy) with request rate limiting before the Spring Boot application.

findings.title: No static machine-readable API specification committed
findings.description: Springfox 3.0.0 generates an OpenAPI specification at runtime (/api-docs), but no committed static OpenAPI specification file exists in the repository for the online service's own API.
findings.gap: No static machine-readable API specification is committed to the repository. Agent tool generation requires a spec file at build time.
findings.recommendation: Add a build step that exports the Springfox-generated OpenAPI spec to a static openapi.yaml file committed to the repository.

compensating_controls: Export the runtime-generated spec from a running instance and commit it to the repository.
Use springdoc-openapi-maven-plugin to generate the spec during build.

findings.title: No structured error responses with retryable classification
findings.description: Default Spring Boot error handling provides HTTP status and message. No consistent structured error response format with error code, message, and retryable classification across all endpoints.
findings.gap: No standardized error response structure with retryable classification to help agents distinguish retriable from terminal failures.
findings.recommendation: Add a global @ControllerAdvice exception handler that returns structured JSON errors with fields: error_code, message, retryable, and details.

compensating_controls: Agents can rely on HTTP status codes (4xx = terminal, 5xx = retriable) as a baseline strategy.
Document error codes in the exported OpenAPI spec for agent tool generation.

findings.title: No distributed tracing or structured logging
findings.description: No distributed tracing (no X-Ray, no OpenTelemetry), no structured JSON logging, no correlation ID propagation. Logging relies on Spring Boot defaults.
findings.gap: Cannot trace agent-initiated requests through the system. No structured logs for automated analysis.
findings.recommendation: Add Spring Cloud Sleuth or Micrometer Tracing and configure Logback with JSON encoder and MDC correlation IDs.

compensating_controls: Add Spring Cloud Sleuth for automatic trace ID injection.
Configure Logback to output JSON format with a request-scoped MDC correlation ID.

findings.title: No alerting on error rates or latency
findings.description: No alerting configuration exists. No CloudWatch alarms, no PagerDuty integration, no health check monitoring, no Spring Boot Actuator.
findings.gap: Service degradation would not be detected until users report failures.
findings.recommendation: Add spring-boot-starter-actuator for metrics exposure, then configure CloudWatch or Prometheus alerting on p99 latency and 5xx error rate.

compensating_controls: Add Spring Boot Actuator with health and metrics endpoints for external monitoring.
Deploy behind an ALB with health check configured.

findings.title: No API versioning or breaking change detection
findings.description: The online service API has no explicit versioning (no /v1/ prefix, no Accept-Version header). No breaking change detection tools configured in CI.
findings.gap: No API versioning, no breaking change detection. Changes to the GenApi interface could break agent tool bindings without notice.
findings.recommendation: Add URL-based versioning (e.g., /v1/api/gen/...) and integrate OpenAPI diff tooling in CI.

compensating_controls: Pin agent tool definitions to a specific API version once versioning is introduced.
Add consumer-driven contract tests (Pact) to detect breaking changes.
